I will trim down into just the claim on an individual thing later[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 01:43, 25 April 2023 (UTC)}} {{ill|Shukuyodo|ja|宿曜道}} is an [[esoteric buddhist]] tradition based on {{ill|astrology|simple}} in [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Kotyk |first=Jeffrey |date=2018-03-22 |title=Japanese Buddhist Astrology and Astral Magic: Mikkyo and Sukuyodo |url=https://go.gale.com/ps/i.do?p=AONE&sw=w&issn=03041042&v=2.1&it=r&id=GALE%7CA547075473&sid=googleScholar&linkaccess=abs |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |language=English |volume=45 |issue=1 |pages=37–87}}</ref> [[Category:dd]][[ja:宿曜道]]
==Deities== This Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Sukuyōdō}}[[category:kk]] [[Category contains the deities {{ill|:Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Sukuyōdō}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Sukuyōdō deities|ja|Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Sukuyōdō}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:宿曜道の神}}
==Onmyōdō as a religion and the deification of onmyōji in the Heian period== After the assassination of [[Fujiwara no Tanetsugu]] in 785, the [[Emperor Kanmu]] was frightened by a [[onryō|vengeful spirit]] of [[Prince Sawara]], his younger brother, due to the frequent incidents of personal disasters and mourning. The relocation of the capital from [[Nagaoka-kyō]] to [[Heian-kyō]] (present-day [[Kyoto]]) by him triggered a sudden spread of belief in [[goryō|noble ghost]] to appease vengeful spirits, especially in the Imperial court, and the tendency to seek more powerful benefits from spellcasting to dispel evil spirits became stronger. Against this backdrop, in addition to the [[Ko-Shintō|ancient Shintoism]], religious beliefs in the stars and Taoist spells, such as those using [[Ofuda|sacred symbols]], came to be the focus of attention. {{ill|Jugon Hakase|lt=Doctor of Spellcasting|ja|呪禁博士}} and {{ill|Jugonshi|lt=spellcasters|ja|呪禁師}} were in charge of {{ill|Jugon (Taoism)|lt=spellcasting|ja|呪禁}}, which had elements of prophecy, [[Taoism]], [[Buddhism]], and especially [[Vajrayana|esotericism]], and belonged to the {{ill|Bureau of Pharmacy|ja|典薬寮}} of the [[Ministry of the Imperial Household]], which had been established as an institution to offer prayers as medical treatment. However, [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]], who was a researcher of onmyōdō, abolished them, and they were integrated into the Bureau of Onmyō. In this way, onmyōdō began to have elements of various colors, from Taoism or Buddhism (especially esoteric Buddhism introduced in the Nara and Heian periods (end of the 8th century)), astrology called {{ill|sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which was introduced along with them, to ancient Shintoism. With the advent of the noble ghost faith, the onmyōdō became even more diverse. For example, spells such as {{ill|Katatagae|lt=changing the direction for good fortune|ja|方違え}} and {{ill|Monoimi|lt=self-consecration|ja|物忌み}}, rituals such as the Festival of the [[Dongyue Dadi|Great Emperor of the Sacred Mountain of the East]], and [[Yu the Great|Uho]] steps (hempai<ref name="kosaka">{{cite magazine |last=Kosaka |first=Shinji |date=6 October 2001 |title=陰陽師が反閇をつとめるとはどういうことか |trans-title=What does it mean for an onmyōji to do hempai? |url=https://ddnavi.com/davinci/backnumber/90/ |magazine=da Vinci |language=ja |location=[[Chiyoda, Tokyo|Chiyoda City]], Tokyo Metropolis, Japan |publisher=[[Kadokawa Future Publishing]] |volume=October, 2001}}</ref>), which were often seen in onmyōdō practices, originated from Taoism, and rice scattering and [[Norito|liturgical incantations]] {{Citation needed span|originated from ancient Shinto.|date=September 2011}} Furthermore, in the process of the [[Hokke (Fujiwara)|Hokke]] of the [[Fujiwara clan]]'s expansion and establishment of power in the Imperial court, political conflicts among nobles intensified, and there were many occasions when onmyōdō was used for slander and defamation aimed at the downfall of rival forces.
This trend became more pronounced with the rise of [[Fujiwara no Yoshifusa]] during the reigns of Emperors [[Emperor Ninmyō|Ninmyō]] and [[Emperor Montoku|Montoku]] (in the middle of the 9th century). [[Emperor Uda]] himself was well versed in the art of I Ching, and [[Fujiwara no Morosuke]] even wrote his own books "Kujō Dono Ikai" and "Kujō Nenchū Gyōji," and presented a guide that incorporated many taboos and manners based on the yin-and-yang philosophy. This environment produced charismatic onmyōji such as {{ill|Shigeoka no Kawahito|ja|滋岳川人}} and {{ill|Yuge no Koreo|ja|弓削是雄}}, and also led to the introduction of a [[regnal year]] following disasters as predicted by the [[Chinese classics]] scholar [[Miyoshi Kiyotsura]], which became a regular event after 901. As a result, onmyōdō became more and more important to the Imperial court. In addition, the fact that people outside the Bureau of Onmyō, such as Fujiwara no Morosuke and Miyoshi Kiyotsura, had mastered astrology, onmyōdō, I Ching, and calendars shows that the classified information policy under the ritsuryō system, which prohibited the leakage of onmyōdō outside the Bureau of Onmyō, had already practically failed by this time.
After the middle of the Heian period, the ritsuryō system was further loosened due to the monopolization of politics by [[Sesshō and Kampaku|Imperial Regents and Chief Imperial Advisors]], and the spread of the [[Shōen|manor system]]. As a result, informal onmyōji, who were not regular government officials and belonged to the Bureau of Onmyō, began to privately associate with the nobles, divining their good and bad fortune, and secretly performing rituals to ward off evil. In some cases, they would even undertake to kill their opponents with curses. Even among the official onmyōji who belonged to the Bureau of Onmyō, there were many who followed this trend. Their behavior was far removed from the duties of onmyōji as originally stipulated by the ritsuryō system. The onmyōji arbitrarily imposed good and bad fortune on the Emperor, Imperial family, senior government positions, and nobles in relation to the fortunate directions, and the movements of the stars, and even entered into the management of their private lives. As onmyōji began to control the spiritual world at the center of the Imperial court, they gradually went beyond their regular duties under the governmental system and began to work behind the scenes of the government. At the same time, there appeared {{ill|Kamo no Tadayuki|ja|賀茂忠行}} and his son, [[Kamo no Yasunori]], as well as their disciple, [[Abe no Seimei]], who were onmyōji well versed in all aspects of astrology, onmyōdō and calendars. They defied precedent and were promoted to even higher ranks, earning the trust of the Imperial court. Kamo no Yasunori taught calendar to his son {{ill|Kamo no Mitsuyoshi|ja|賀茂光栄}} and astrology to his disciple Abe no Seimei. They passed on these knowledge and skills only to the children of their own families and forbade teaching them to others. The astrology of the Abe family took on the nature of preaching disasters and good omens, while the calendars of the Kamo family took on a strong astrological flavor. For this reason, only the Kamo and Abe families produced onmyōji. When Abe no Seimei's grandson, Abe no Akichika, became the Head of Onmyō, he expressed his policy of always appointing people from the Kamo family as Doctor of Calendar and people from the Abe family as Doctor of Astrology. After that, the two families almost monopolized other positions in the Bureau of Onmyō that were not originally meant to be inherited by the two families. In addition, even though their actual status was that of onmyōji, they came to hold other higher official positions beyond the duties of the Bureau of Onmyō, and the bureau as a governmental system became a complete skeleton. The onmyōji became a charismatic spiritual ruler in the Imperial court with a strong tinge of religious spells and rituals, and came to wield a powerful influence. From the middle of the Heian period onward, onmyōji had a great influence on the central government of the Imperial court, from political management and personnel decisions to the abdication of the Emperor. Onmyōji were also deeply involved in the 901 incident in which [[Fujiwara no Tokihira]], the [[Minister of the Left]], relegated [[Sugawara no Michizane]] from the position of [[Minister of the Right]] to the position of deputy commissioner of [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]], the regional government in [[Chikuzen Province]].
It was also around this time that many onmyōji began to be seen in local areas outside of Heian-kyō, the capital at the time, although their activities as onmyōji outside of the Bureau of Onmyō were originally prohibited by the ritsuryō system. In the local areas, many private onmyōji appeared, including the {{ill|Dōma Hōshi|lt=Reverend Dōma|ja|道摩法師}} (well known as Ashiya Dōman).
Throughout the middle and latter half of the Heian period ([[11th century|11th]] to [[12th century|12th centuries]]), the Abe family produced many masters in astrology, which was the most difficult of all the duties of the Bureau of Onmyō, and the Abe family always succeeded as a Head of Onmyō, while the Kamo family succeeded as a Vice Head of Onmyō. At the time of the [[Genpei War]] at the end of the Heian period, Abe no Yasuchika, the grandson of {{ill|Abe no Yoshihira|ja|安倍吉平}}, the son of Abe no Seimei, and Abe no Suehiro, the son of {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}, held particularly high official ranks. However, due to the loss of political power that accompanied the subsequent transfer of power to the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the turmoil within the Abe family caused by the struggle for power between the [[Northern Court|Northern]] and [[Southern Court]]s at the end of the [[Kamakura period]], and the subsequent disorder during the [[Nanboku-chō period]], the power of the Abe family temporarily declined.

=== Manga and anime ===
* ''[[Tokyo Babylon]]'', written by [[Clamp (manga artists)|Clamp]], published in 1990 * ''[[X (manga)|X]]'', written by Clamp, published in 1992 * ''[[Shaman King]]'', written by [[Hiroyuki Takei]], published in 1998 * ''[[Gag Manga Biyori]]'', written by [[Kōsuke Masuda]], published in 2000 * ''[[Magical Shopping Arcade Abenobashi]]'', created by [[Gainax]], published in 2001 * ''[[Amatsuki]]'', written by {{ill|Shinobu Takayama|ja|高山しのぶ}}, published in 2002 * ''[[Gin Tama]]'', written by [[Hideaki Sorachi]], published in 2003 * ''[[xxxHolic]]'', written by Clamp, published in 2003 * ''[[Onmyō Taisenki]]'', written by Yoshihiko Tomizawa, published in 2003 * ''[[Musashi Gundoh]]'', based on an unused story by [[Monkey Punch]], published in 2006 * ''{{ill|Shibariya Komachi|ja|縛り屋小町}}'', written by [[Mick Takeuchi]], published in 2006 * ''[[Nura: Rise of the Yokai Clan]]'', written by [[Hiroshi Shiibashi]], published in 2008 * ''[[Twin Star Exorcists]]'', written by {{ill|Yoshiaki Sukeno|ja|助野嘉昭}}, published in 2013 * ''[[The Reincarnation of the Strongest Exorcist in Another World]]'', written by Kiichi Kosuzu, published in 2018 * ''[[MAO (manga)|MAO]]'', written by [[Rumiko Takahashi]], published in 2019
=== Films === *''[[Tokyo: The Last Megalopolis]]'', a 1988 Japanese fantasy film directed by [[Akio Jissōji]], based on the novel ''[[Teito Monogatari]]'' by Hiroshi Aramata *[[Onmyōji (film)|''Onmyōji'' (film)]], a 2001 Japanese film based on the [[Onmyōji (novel series)|''Onmyōji'' (novel series)]], by Baku Yumemakura and released in the U.S. in 2003 as ''Onmyōji - The Yin Yang Master'' '''''[[Onmyōji 2]]'', the 2003 sequel *''[[The Yin-Yang Master: Dream of Eternity]]'', a 2020 Chinese fantasy film based on the [[Onmyōji (novel series)|''Onmyōji'' (novel series)]], by Baku Yumemakura directed by [[Guo Jingming]], and starring [[Mark Chao]] and [[Deng Lun]] *''[[The Yinyang Master]]'', a 2021 Chinese fantasy film directed by Li Weiran, and starring [[Chen Kun]], [[Zhou Xun]] and [[William Chan]] based on the game that was based on the [[Onmyōji (novel series)|''Onmyōji'' (novel series)]], by Baku Yumemakura
=== Video games === * ''{{ill|Kuon no Kizuna|ja|久遠の絆}}'', developed by [[FOG Inc.]], released in 1998 * ''[[Tokyo Majin Gakuen]]'', produced by {{ill|Shūhō Imai|ja|今井秋芳}}, released in 1998 * ''[[Kuon]]'', developed by [[FromSoftware]], released in 2004 * ''[[11eyes: Tsumi to Batsu to Aganai no Shōjo]]'', developed by {{ill|Lass (video game developer)|lt=Lass|ja|Lass}}, released in 2008 * ''[[Naraka: Bladepoint]]'', developed by [[24 Entertainment]], released in 2021 features the character [[Kurumi Tsuchimikado]], who according to game lore is the first female Onmyōji, descended from a long line of Onmyōdō masters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.narakathegame.com/index.html|title = Naraka: Bladepoint - New Hero Out Now!}}</ref>

{{draft}} {{AFC comment|1=Note: This draft started from a complete copy of [[Onmyōji]] as it has a lot of redlinks with claims I want to keep. I will trim down into just the claim on an individual thing later[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 01:43, 25 April 2023 (UTC)}} {{ill|Shukuyodo|ja|宿曜道}} is an [[esoteric buddhist]] tradition based on {{ill|astrology|simple}} in [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Kotyk |first=Jeffrey |date=2018-03-22 |title=Japanese Buddhist Astrology and Astral Magic: Mikkyo and Sukuyodo |url=https://go.gale.com/ps/i.do?p=AONE&sw=w&issn=03041042&v=2.1&it=r&id=GALE%7CA547075473&sid=googleScholar&linkaccess=abs |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |language=English |volume=45 |issue=1 |pages=37–87}}</ref> [[Category:dd]][[ja:宿曜道]]
==Deities== This Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Sukuyōdō}}[[category:kk]] [[Category contains the deities {{ill|:Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Sukuyōdō}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Sukuyōdō deities|ja|Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Sukuyōdō}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:宿曜道の神}}
==Onmyōdō as a religion and the deification of onmyōji in the Heian period== After the assassination of [[Fujiwara no Tanetsugu]] in 785, the [[Emperor Kanmu]] was frightened by a [[onryō|vengeful spirit]] of [[Prince Sawara]], his younger brother, due to the frequent incidents of personal disasters and mourning. The relocation of the capital from [[Nagaoka-kyō]] to [[Heian-kyō]] (present-day [[Kyoto]]) by him triggered a sudden spread of belief in [[goryō|noble ghost]] to appease vengeful spirits, especially in the Imperial court, and the tendency to seek more powerful benefits from spellcasting to dispel evil spirits became stronger. Against this backdrop, in addition to the [[Ko-Shintō|ancient Shintoism]], religious beliefs in the stars and Taoist spells, such as those using [[Ofuda|sacred symbols]], came to be the focus of attention. {{ill|Jugon Hakase|lt=Doctor of Spellcasting|ja|呪禁博士}} and {{ill|Jugonshi|lt=spellcasters|ja|呪禁師}} were in charge of {{ill|Jugon (Taoism)|lt=spellcasting|ja|呪禁}}, which had elements of prophecy, [[Taoism]], [[Buddhism]], and especially [[Vajrayana|esotericism]], and belonged to the {{ill|Bureau of Pharmacy|ja|典薬寮}} of the [[Ministry of the Imperial Household]], which had been established as an institution to offer prayers as medical treatment. However, [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]], who was a researcher of onmyōdō, abolished them, and they were integrated into the Bureau of Onmyō. In this way, onmyōdō began to have elements of various colors, from Taoism or Buddhism (especially esoteric Buddhism introduced in the Nara and Heian periods (end of the 8th century)), astrology called {{ill|sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which was introduced along with them, to ancient Shintoism. With the advent of the noble ghost faith, the onmyōdō became even more diverse. For example, spells such as {{ill|Katatagae|lt=changing the direction for good fortune|ja|方違え}} and {{ill|Monoimi|lt=self-consecration|ja|物忌み}}, rituals such as the Festival of the [[Dongyue Dadi|Great Emperor of the Sacred Mountain of the East]], and [[Yu the Great|Uho]] steps (hempai<ref name="kosaka">{{cite magazine |last=Kosaka |first=Shinji |date=6 October 2001 |title=陰陽師が反閇をつとめるとはどういうことか |trans-title=What does it mean for an onmyōji to do hempai? |url=https://ddnavi.com/davinci/backnumber/90/ |magazine=da Vinci |language=ja |location=[[Chiyoda, Tokyo|Chiyoda City]], Tokyo Metropolis, Japan |publisher=[[Kadokawa Future Publishing]] |volume=October, 2001}}</ref>), which were often seen in onmyōdō practices, originated from Taoism, and rice scattering and [[Norito|liturgical incantations]] {{Citation needed span|originated from ancient Shinto.|date=September 2011}} Furthermore, in the process of the [[Hokke (Fujiwara)|Hokke]] of the [[Fujiwara clan]]'s expansion and establishment of power in the Imperial court, political conflicts among nobles intensified, and there were many occasions when onmyōdō was used for slander and defamation aimed at the downfall of rival forces.
This trend became more pronounced with the rise of [[Fujiwara no Yoshifusa]] during the reigns of Emperors [[Emperor Ninmyō|Ninmyō]] and [[Emperor Montoku|Montoku]] (in the middle of the 9th century). [[Emperor Uda]] himself was well versed in the art of I Ching, and [[Fujiwara no Morosuke]] even wrote his own books "Kujō Dono Ikai" and "Kujō Nenchū Gyōji," and presented a guide that incorporated many taboos and manners based on the yin-and-yang philosophy. This environment produced charismatic onmyōji such as {{ill|Shigeoka no Kawahito|ja|滋岳川人}} and {{ill|Yuge no Koreo|ja|弓削是雄}}, and also led to the introduction of a [[regnal year]] following disasters as predicted by the [[Chinese classics]] scholar [[Miyoshi Kiyotsura]], which became a regular event after 901. As a result, onmyōdō became more and more important to the Imperial court. In addition, the fact that people outside the Bureau of Onmyō, such as Fujiwara no Morosuke and Miyoshi Kiyotsura, had mastered astrology, onmyōdō, I Ching, and calendars shows that the classified information policy under the ritsuryō system, which prohibited the leakage of onmyōdō outside the Bureau of Onmyō, had already practically failed by this time.
After the middle of the Heian period, the ritsuryō system was further loosened due to the monopolization of politics by [[Sesshō and Kampaku|Imperial Regents and Chief Imperial Advisors]], and the spread of the [[Shōen|manor system]]. As a result, informal onmyōji, who were not regular government officials and belonged to the Bureau of Onmyō, began to privately associate with the nobles, divining their good and bad fortune, and secretly performing rituals to ward off evil. In some cases, they would even undertake to kill their opponents with curses. Even among the official onmyōji who belonged to the Bureau of Onmyō, there were many who followed this trend. Their behavior was far removed from the duties of onmyōji as originally stipulated by the ritsuryō system. The onmyōji arbitrarily imposed good and bad fortune on the Emperor, Imperial family, senior government positions, and nobles in relation to the fortunate directions, and the movements of the stars, and even entered into the management of their private lives. As onmyōji began to control the spiritual world at the center of the Imperial court, they gradually went beyond their regular duties under the governmental system and began to work behind the scenes of the government. At the same time, there appeared {{ill|Kamo no Tadayuki|ja|賀茂忠行}} and his son, [[Kamo no Yasunori]], as well as their disciple, [[Abe no Seimei]], who were onmyōji well versed in all aspects of astrology, onmyōdō and calendars. They defied precedent and were promoted to even higher ranks, earning the trust of the Imperial court. Kamo no Yasunori taught calendar to his son {{ill|Kamo no Mitsuyoshi|ja|賀茂光栄}} and astrology to his disciple Abe no Seimei. They passed on these knowledge and skills only to the children of their own families and forbade teaching them to others. The astrology of the Abe family took on the nature of preaching disasters and good omens, while the calendars of the Kamo family took on a strong astrological flavor. For this reason, only the Kamo and Abe families produced onmyōji. When Abe no Seimei's grandson, Abe no Akichika, became the Head of Onmyō, he expressed his policy of always appointing people from the Kamo family as Doctor of Calendar and people from the Abe family as Doctor of Astrology. After that, the two families almost monopolized other positions in the Bureau of Onmyō that were not originally meant to be inherited by the two families. In addition, even though their actual status was that of onmyōji, they came to hold other higher official positions beyond the duties of the Bureau of Onmyō, and the bureau as a governmental system became a complete skeleton. The onmyōji became a charismatic spiritual ruler in the Imperial court with a strong tinge of religious spells and rituals, and came to wield a powerful influence. From the middle of the Heian period onward, onmyōji had a great influence on the central government of the Imperial court, from political management and personnel decisions to the abdication of the Emperor. Onmyōji were also deeply involved in the 901 incident in which [[Fujiwara no Tokihira]], the [[Minister of the Left]], relegated [[Sugawara no Michizane]] from the position of [[Minister of the Right]] to the position of deputy commissioner of [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]], the regional government in [[Chikuzen Province]].
It was also around this time that many onmyōji began to be seen in local areas outside of Heian-kyō, the capital at the time, although their activities as onmyōji outside of the Bureau of Onmyō were originally prohibited by the ritsuryō system. In the local areas, many private onmyōji appeared, including the {{ill|Dōma Hōshi|lt=Reverend Dōma|ja|道摩法師}} (well known as Ashiya Dōman).
Throughout the middle and latter half of the Heian period ([[11th century|11th]] to [[12th century|12th centuries]]), the Abe family produced many masters in astrology, which was the most difficult of all the duties of the Bureau of Onmyō, and the Abe family always succeeded as a Head of Onmyō, while the Kamo family succeeded as a Vice Head of Onmyō. At the time of the [[Genpei War]] at the end of the Heian period, Abe no Yasuchika, the grandson of {{ill|Abe no Yoshihira|ja|安倍吉平}}, the son of Abe no Seimei, and Abe no Suehiro, the son of {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}, held particularly high official ranks. However, due to the loss of political power that accompanied the subsequent transfer of power to the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the turmoil within the Abe family caused by the struggle for power between the [[Northern Court|Northern]] and [[Southern Court]]s at the end of the [[Kamakura period]], and the subsequent disorder during the [[Nanboku-chō period]], the power of the Abe family temporarily declined.
==In media== === Books === * ''[[Teito Monogatari]]'', written by [[Hiroshi Aramata]], published in 1985 * ''[[Onmyōji (novel series)|Onmyōji]]'', written by [[Baku Yumemakura]], published in 1988 * ''[[The Summer of the Ubume]]'', ''[[Mōryō no Hako]]'' and other Kyōgokudō series, written by [[Natsuhiko Kyogoku]], published in 1994 * ''{{ill|Onmyō no Miyako|ja|陰陽ノ京}}'', written by [[Soichiro Watase]], published in 2001 * ''[[Shōnen Onmyōji]]'', written by [[Mitsuru Yūki]], published in 2001 * ''[[Rental Magica]]'', written by {{ill|Makoto Sanda|ja|三田誠}}, published in 2004 * ''{{ill|Bakemonogatari|ja|化物語}}'', written by [[Nisio Isin]], published in 2005 * ''[[Fairy Navigator Runa]]'', written by {{ill|Miyoko Ikeda|ja|池田美代子}}, published in 2008 * ''[[Tokyo Ravens]]'', written by {{ill|Kōhei Azano|ja|あざの耕平}}, published in 2010
=== Manga and anime ===
* ''[[Tokyo Babylon]]'', written by [[Clamp (manga artists)|Clamp]], published in 1990 * ''[[X (manga)|X]]'', written by Clamp, published in 1992 * ''[[Shaman King]]'', written by [[Hiroyuki Takei]], published in 1998 * ''[[Gag Manga Biyori]]'', written by [[Kōsuke Masuda]], published in 2000 * ''[[Magical Shopping Arcade Abenobashi]]'', created by [[Gainax]], published in 2001 * ''[[Amatsuki]]'', written by {{ill|Shinobu Takayama|ja|高山しのぶ}}, published in 2002 * ''[[Gin Tama]]'', written by [[Hideaki Sorachi]], published in 2003 * ''[[xxxHolic]]'', written by Clamp, published in 2003 * ''[[Onmyō Taisenki]]'', written by Yoshihiko Tomizawa, published in 2003 * ''[[Musashi Gundoh]]'', based on an unused story by [[Monkey Punch]], published in 2006 * ''{{ill|Shibariya Komachi|ja|縛り屋小町}}'', written by [[Mick Takeuchi]], published in 2006 * ''[[Nura: Rise of the Yokai Clan]]'', written by [[Hiroshi Shiibashi]], published in 2008 * ''[[Twin Star Exorcists]]'', written by {{ill|Yoshiaki Sukeno|ja|助野嘉昭}}, published in 2013 * ''[[The Reincarnation of the Strongest Exorcist in Another World]]'', written by Kiichi Kosuzu, published in 2018 * ''[[MAO (manga)|MAO]]'', written by [[Rumiko Takahashi]], published in 2019
=== Films === *''[[Tokyo: The Last Megalopolis]]'', a 1988 Japanese fantasy film directed by [[Akio Jissōji]], based on the novel ''[[Teito Monogatari]]'' by Hiroshi Aramata *[[Onmyōji (film)|''Onmyōji'' (film)]], a 2001 Japanese film based on the [[Onmyōji (novel series)|''Onmyōji'' (novel series)]], by Baku Yumemakura and released in the U.S. in 2003 as ''Onmyōji - The Yin Yang Master'' '''''[[Onmyōji 2]]'', the 2003 sequel *''[[The Yin-Yang Master: Dream of Eternity]]'', a 2020 Chinese fantasy film based on the [[Onmyōji (novel series)|''Onmyōji'' (novel series)]], by Baku Yumemakura directed by [[Guo Jingming]], and starring [[Mark Chao]] and [[Deng Lun]] *''[[The Yinyang Master]]'', a 2021 Chinese fantasy film directed by Li Weiran, and starring [[Chen Kun]], [[Zhou Xun]] and [[William Chan]] based on the game that was based on the [[Onmyōji (novel series)|''Onmyōji'' (novel series)]], by Baku Yumemakura
=== Video games === * ''{{ill|Kuon no Kizuna|ja|久遠の絆}}'', developed by [[FOG Inc.]], released in 1998 * ''[[Tokyo Majin Gakuen]]'', produced by {{ill|Shūhō Imai|ja|今井秋芳}}, released in 1998 * ''[[Kuon]]'', developed by [[FromSoftware]], released in 2004 * ''[[11eyes: Tsumi to Batsu to Aganai no Shōjo]]'', developed by {{ill|Lass (video game developer)|lt=Lass|ja|Lass}}, released in 2008 * ''[[Naraka: Bladepoint]]'', developed by [[24 Entertainment]], released in 2021 features the character [[Kurumi Tsuchimikado]], who according to game lore is the first female Onmyōji, descended from a long line of Onmyōdō masters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.narakathegame.com/index.html|title = Naraka: Bladepoint - New Hero Out Now!}}</ref>

{{draft}} {{AFC comment|1=Note: This draft started from a complete copy of [[Onmyōji]] as it has a lot of redlinks with claims I want to keep. I will trim down into just the claim on an individual thing later[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 01:43, 25 April 2023 (UTC)}} {{ill|Shukuyodo|ja|宿曜道}} is an [[esoteric buddhist]] tradition based on {{ill|astrology|simple}} in [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Kotyk |first=Jeffrey |date=2018-03-22 |title=Japanese Buddhist Astrology and Astral Magic: Mikkyo and Sukuyodo |url=https://go.gale.com/ps/i.do?p=AONE&sw=w&issn=03041042&v=2.1&it=r&id=GALE%7CA547075473&sid=googleScholar&linkaccess=abs |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |language=English |volume=45 |issue=1 |pages=37–87}}</ref> [[Category:dd]][[ja:宿曜道]]
==Deities== This Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Sukuyōdō}}[[category:kk]] [[Category contains the deities {{ill|:Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Sukuyōdō}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Sukuyōdō deities|ja|Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Sukuyōdō}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:宿曜道の神}}
==Onmyōdō as a religion and the deification of onmyōji in the Heian period== After the assassination of [[Fujiwara no Tanetsugu]] in 785, the [[Emperor Kanmu]] was frightened by a [[onryō|vengeful spirit]] of [[Prince Sawara]], his younger brother, due to the frequent incidents of personal disasters and mourning. The relocation of the capital from [[Nagaoka-kyō]] to [[Heian-kyō]] (present-day [[Kyoto]]) by him triggered a sudden spread of belief in [[goryō|noble ghost]] to appease vengeful spirits, especially in the Imperial court, and the tendency to seek more powerful benefits from spellcasting to dispel evil spirits became stronger. Against this backdrop, in addition to the [[Ko-Shintō|ancient Shintoism]], religious beliefs in the stars and Taoist spells, such as those using [[Ofuda|sacred symbols]], came to be the focus of attention. {{ill|Jugon Hakase|lt=Doctor of Spellcasting|ja|呪禁博士}} and {{ill|Jugonshi|lt=spellcasters|ja|呪禁師}} were in charge of {{ill|Jugon (Taoism)|lt=spellcasting|ja|呪禁}}, which had elements of prophecy, [[Taoism]], [[Buddhism]], and especially [[Vajrayana|esotericism]], and belonged to the {{ill|Bureau of Pharmacy|ja|典薬寮}} of the [[Ministry of the Imperial Household]], which had been established as an institution to offer prayers as medical treatment. However, [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]], who was a researcher of onmyōdō, abolished them, and they were integrated into the Bureau of Onmyō. In this way, onmyōdō began to have elements of various colors, from Taoism or Buddhism (especially esoteric Buddhism introduced in the Nara and Heian periods (end of the 8th century)), astrology called {{ill|sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which was introduced along with them, to ancient Shintoism. With the advent of the noble ghost faith, the onmyōdō became even more diverse. For example, spells such as {{ill|Katatagae|lt=changing the direction for good fortune|ja|方違え}} and {{ill|Monoimi|lt=self-consecration|ja|物忌み}}, rituals such as the Festival of the [[Dongyue Dadi|Great Emperor of the Sacred Mountain of the East]], and [[Yu the Great|Uho]] steps (hempai<ref name="kosaka">{{cite magazine |last=Kosaka |first=Shinji |date=6 October 2001 |title=陰陽師が反閇をつとめるとはどういうことか |trans-title=What does it mean for an onmyōji to do hempai? |url=https://ddnavi.com/davinci/backnumber/90/ |magazine=da Vinci |language=ja |location=[[Chiyoda, Tokyo|Chiyoda City]], Tokyo Metropolis, Japan |publisher=[[Kadokawa Future Publishing]] |volume=October, 2001}}</ref>), which were often seen in onmyōdō practices, originated from Taoism, and rice scattering and [[Norito|liturgical incantations]] {{Citation needed span|originated from ancient Shinto.|date=September 2011}} Furthermore, in the process of the [[Hokke (Fujiwara)|Hokke]] of the [[Fujiwara clan]]'s expansion and establishment of power in the Imperial court, political conflicts among nobles intensified, and there were many occasions when onmyōdō was used for slander and defamation aimed at the downfall of rival forces.
This trend became more pronounced with the rise of [[Fujiwara no Yoshifusa]] during the reigns of Emperors [[Emperor Ninmyō|Ninmyō]] and [[Emperor Montoku|Montoku]] (in the middle of the 9th century). [[Emperor Uda]] himself was well versed in the art of I Ching, and [[Fujiwara no Morosuke]] even wrote his own books "Kujō Dono Ikai" and "Kujō Nenchū Gyōji," and presented a guide that incorporated many taboos and manners based on the yin-and-yang philosophy. This environment produced charismatic onmyōji such as {{ill|Shigeoka no Kawahito|ja|滋岳川人}} and {{ill|Yuge no Koreo|ja|弓削是雄}}, and also led to the introduction of a [[regnal year]] following disasters as predicted by the [[Chinese classics]] scholar [[Miyoshi Kiyotsura]], which became a regular event after 901. As a result, onmyōdō became more and more important to the Imperial court. In addition, the fact that people outside the Bureau of Onmyō, such as Fujiwara no Morosuke and Miyoshi Kiyotsura, had mastered astrology, onmyōdō, I Ching, and calendars shows that the classified information policy under the ritsuryō system, which prohibited the leakage of onmyōdō outside the Bureau of Onmyō, had already practically failed by this time.
After the middle of the Heian period, the ritsuryō system was further loosened due to the monopolization of politics by [[Sesshō and Kampaku|Imperial Regents and Chief Imperial Advisors]], and the spread of the [[Shōen|manor system]]. As a result, informal onmyōji, who were not regular government officials and belonged to the Bureau of Onmyō, began to privately associate with the nobles, divining their good and bad fortune, and secretly performing rituals to ward off evil. In some cases, they would even undertake to kill their opponents with curses. Even among the official onmyōji who belonged to the Bureau of Onmyō, there were many who followed this trend. Their behavior was far removed from the duties of onmyōji as originally stipulated by the ritsuryō system. The onmyōji arbitrarily imposed good and bad fortune on the Emperor, Imperial family, senior government positions, and nobles in relation to the fortunate directions, and the movements of the stars, and even entered into the management of their private lives. As onmyōji began to control the spiritual world at the center of the Imperial court, they gradually went beyond their regular duties under the governmental system and began to work behind the scenes of the government. At the same time, there appeared {{ill|Kamo no Tadayuki|ja|賀茂忠行}} and his son, [[Kamo no Yasunori]], as well as their disciple, [[Abe no Seimei]], who were onmyōji well versed in all aspects of astrology, onmyōdō and calendars. They defied precedent and were promoted to even higher ranks, earning the trust of the Imperial court. Kamo no Yasunori taught calendar to his son {{ill|Kamo no Mitsuyoshi|ja|賀茂光栄}} and astrology to his disciple Abe no Seimei. They passed on these knowledge and skills only to the children of their own families and forbade teaching them to others. The astrology of the Abe family took on the nature of preaching disasters and good omens, while the calendars of the Kamo family took on a strong astrological flavor. For this reason, only the Kamo and Abe families produced onmyōji. When Abe no Seimei's grandson, Abe no Akichika, became the Head of Onmyō, he expressed his policy of always appointing people from the Kamo family as Doctor of Calendar and people from the Abe family as Doctor of Astrology. After that, the two families almost monopolized other positions in the Bureau of Onmyō that were not originally meant to be inherited by the two families. In addition, even though their actual status was that of onmyōji, they came to hold other higher official positions beyond the duties of the Bureau of Onmyō, and the bureau as a governmental system became a complete skeleton. The onmyōji became a charismatic spiritual ruler in the Imperial court with a strong tinge of religious spells and rituals, and came to wield a powerful influence. From the middle of the Heian period onward, onmyōji had a great influence on the central government of the Imperial court, from political management and personnel decisions to the abdication of the Emperor. Onmyōji were also deeply involved in the 901 incident in which [[Fujiwara no Tokihira]], the [[Minister of the Left]], relegated [[Sugawara no Michizane]] from the position of [[Minister of the Right]] to the position of deputy commissioner of [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]], the regional government in [[Chikuzen Province]].
It was also around this time that many onmyōji began to be seen in local areas outside of Heian-kyō, the capital at the time, although their activities as onmyōji outside of the Bureau of Onmyō were originally prohibited by the ritsuryō system. In the local areas, many private onmyōji appeared, including the {{ill|Dōma Hōshi|lt=Reverend Dōma|ja|道摩法師}} (well known as Ashiya Dōman).
Throughout the middle and latter half of the Heian period ([[11th century|11th]] to [[12th century|12th centuries]]), the Abe family produced many masters in astrology, which was the most difficult of all the duties of the Bureau of Onmyō, and the Abe family always succeeded as a Head of Onmyō, while the Kamo family succeeded as a Vice Head of Onmyō. At the time of the [[Genpei War]] at the end of the Heian period, Abe no Yasuchika, the grandson of {{ill|Abe no Yoshihira|ja|安倍吉平}}, the son of Abe no Seimei, and Abe no Suehiro, the son of {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}, held particularly high official ranks. However, due to the loss of political power that accompanied the subsequent transfer of power to the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the turmoil within the Abe family caused by the struggle for power between the [[Northern Court|Northern]] and [[Southern Court]]s at the end of the [[Kamakura period]], and the subsequent disorder during the [[Nanboku-chō period]], the power of the Abe family temporarily declined.
==In media== === Books === * ''[[Teito Monogatari]]'', written by [[Hiroshi Aramata]], published in 1985 * ''[[Onmyōji (novel series)|Onmyōji]]'', written by [[Baku Yumemakura]], published in 1988 * ''[[The Summer of the Ubume]]'', ''[[Mōryō no Hako]]'' and other Kyōgokudō series, written by [[Natsuhiko Kyogoku]], published in 1994 * ''{{ill|Onmyō no Miyako|ja|陰陽ノ京}}'', written by [[Soichiro Watase]], published in 2001 * ''[[Shōnen Onmyōji]]'', written by [[Mitsuru Yūki]], published in 2001 * ''[[Rental Magica]]'', written by {{ill|Makoto Sanda|ja|三田誠}}, published in 2004 * ''{{ill|Bakemonogatari|ja|化物語}}'', written by [[Nisio Isin]], published in 2005 * ''[[Fairy Navigator Runa]]'', written by {{ill|Miyoko Ikeda|ja|池田美代子}}, published in 2008 * ''[[Tokyo Ravens]]'', written by {{ill|Kōhei Azano|ja|あざの耕平}}, published in 2010
=== Manga and anime ===
* ''[[Tokyo Babylon]]'', written by [[Clamp (manga artists)|Clamp]], published in 1990 * ''[[X (manga)|X]]'', written by Clamp, published in 1992 * ''[[Shaman King]]'', written by [[Hiroyuki Takei]], published in 1998 * ''[[Gag Manga Biyori]]'', written by [[Kōsuke Masuda]], published in 2000 * ''[[Magical Shopping Arcade Abenobashi]]'', created by [[Gainax]], published in 2001 * ''[[Amatsuki]]'', written by {{ill|Shinobu Takayama|ja|高山しのぶ}}, published in 2002 * ''[[Gin Tama]]'', written by [[Hideaki Sorachi]], published in 2003 * ''[[xxxHolic]]'', written by Clamp, published in 2003 * ''[[Onmyō Taisenki]]'', written by Yoshihiko Tomizawa, published in 2003 * ''[[Musashi Gundoh]]'', based on an unused story by [[Monkey Punch]], published in 2006 * ''{{ill|Shibariya Komachi|ja|縛り屋小町}}'', written by [[Mick Takeuchi]], published in 2006 * ''[[Nura: Rise of the Yokai Clan]]'', written by [[Hiroshi Shiibashi]], published in 2008 * ''[[Twin Star Exorcists]]'', written by {{ill|Yoshiaki Sukeno|ja|助野嘉昭}}, published in 2013 * ''[[The Reincarnation of the Strongest Exorcist in Another World]]'', written by Kiichi Kosuzu, published in 2018 * ''[[MAO (manga)|MAO]]'', written by [[Rumiko Takahashi]], published in 2019
=== Films === *''[[Tokyo: The Last Megalopolis]]'', a 1988 Japanese fantasy film directed by [[Akio Jissōji]], based on the novel ''[[Teito Monogatari]]'' by Hiroshi Aramata *[[Onmyōji (film)|''Onmyōji'' (film)]], a 2001 Japanese film based on the [[Onmyōji (novel series)|''Onmyōji'' (novel series)]], by Baku Yumemakura and released in the U.S. in 2003 as ''Onmyōji - The Yin Yang Master'' '''''[[Onmyōji 2]]'', the 2003 sequel *''[[The Yin-Yang Master: Dream of Eternity]]'', a 2020 Chinese fantasy film based on the [[Onmyōji (novel series)|''Onmyōji'' (novel series)]], by Baku Yumemakura directed by [[Guo Jingming]], and starring [[Mark Chao]] and [[Deng Lun]] *''[[The Yinyang Master]]'', a 2021 Chinese fantasy film directed by Li Weiran, and starring [[Chen Kun]], [[Zhou Xun]] and [[William Chan]] based on the game that was based on the [[Onmyōji (novel series)|''Onmyōji'' (novel series)]], by Baku Yumemakura
=== Video games === * ''{{ill|Kuon no Kizuna|ja|久遠の絆}}'', developed by [[FOG Inc.]], released in 1998 * ''[[Tokyo Majin Gakuen]]'', produced by {{ill|Shūhō Imai|ja|今井秋芳}}, released in 1998 * ''[[Kuon]]'', developed by [[FromSoftware]], released in 2004 * ''[[11eyes: Tsumi to Batsu to Aganai no Shōjo]]'', developed by {{ill|Lass (video game developer)|lt=Lass|ja|Lass}}, released in 2008 * ''[[Naraka: Bladepoint]]'', developed by [[24 Entertainment]], released in 2021 features the character [[Kurumi Tsuchimikado]], who according to game lore is the first female Onmyōji, descended from a long line of Onmyōdō masters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.narakathegame.com/index.html|title = Naraka: Bladepoint - New Hero Out Now!}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}} {{Reading_kana|'''Shukuyōdō'''|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Lunar mansions|ja|二十八宿}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Shukuyōkyō|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Shukuyōkyō|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.
== References == {{reflist}}

revision.comment: some pages here lack the tag but to my knowledge everything here eitherr is an original page or at least worth preserving while I potentially trirm the wikipedia derived articles
revision.text:
{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}} {{Reading_kana|'''Shukuyōdō'''|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Lunar mansions|ja|二十八宿}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Shukuyōkyō|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Shukuyōkyō|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} * {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}} * {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり}}
== External Links == * {{kotobank|2=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|宿曜道}} * [https://yakumoin.net/宿曜占星術 宿曜占星術 八雲院] * [https://fs-astrology-members.com/宿曜占星術 宿曜占星術+news] * [https://syukuyo.com/宿曜秘宝協会 宿曜秘宝協会] * [https://kosei-do.co.jp/fortune/uranai.php 宿曜占星術 光晴堂]

{{draft}} {{AFC comment|1=Note: This draft started from a complete copy of [[Onmyōji]] as it has a lot of redlinks with claims I want to keep. I will trim down into just the claim on an individual thing later[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 01:43, 25 April 2023 (UTC)}} {{ill|Shukuyodo|ja|宿曜道}} is an [[esoteric buddhist]] tradition based on {{ill|astrology|simple}} in [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Kotyk |first=Jeffrey |date=2018-03-22 |title=Japanese Buddhist Astrology and Astral Magic: Mikkyo and Sukuyodo |url=https://go.gale.com/ps/i.do?p=AONE&sw=w&issn=03041042&v=2.1&it=r&id=GALE%7CA547075473&sid=googleScholar&linkaccess=abs |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |language=English |volume=45 |issue=1 |pages=37–87}}</ref> [[Category:dd]][[ja:宿曜道]]
==Deities== This Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Sukuyōdō}}[[category:kk]] [[Category contains the deities {{ill|:Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Sukuyōdō}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Sukuyōdō deities|ja|Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Sukuyōdō}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:宿曜道の神}}
==Onmyōdō as a religion and the deification of onmyōji in the Heian period== After the assassination of [[Fujiwara no Tanetsugu]] in 785, the [[Emperor Kanmu]] was frightened by a [[onryō|vengeful spirit]] of [[Prince Sawara]], his younger brother, due to the frequent incidents of personal disasters and mourning. The relocation of the capital from [[Nagaoka-kyō]] to [[Heian-kyō]] (present-day [[Kyoto]]) by him triggered a sudden spread of belief in [[goryō|noble ghost]] to appease vengeful spirits, especially in the Imperial court, and the tendency to seek more powerful benefits from spellcasting to dispel evil spirits became stronger. Against this backdrop, in addition to the [[Ko-Shintō|ancient Shintoism]], religious beliefs in the stars and Taoist spells, such as those using [[Ofuda|sacred symbols]], came to be the focus of attention. {{ill|Jugon Hakase|lt=Doctor of Spellcasting|ja|呪禁博士}} and {{ill|Jugonshi|lt=spellcasters|ja|呪禁師}} were in charge of {{ill|Jugon (Taoism)|lt=spellcasting|ja|呪禁}}, which had elements of prophecy, [[Taoism]], [[Buddhism]], and especially [[Vajrayana|esotericism]], and belonged to the {{ill|Bureau of Pharmacy|ja|典薬寮}} of the [[Ministry of the Imperial Household]], which had been established as an institution to offer prayers as medical treatment. However, [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]], who was a researcher of onmyōdō, abolished them, and they were integrated into the Bureau of Onmyō. In this way, onmyōdō began to have elements of various colors, from Taoism or Buddhism (especially esoteric Buddhism introduced in the Nara and Heian periods (end of the 8th century)), astrology called {{ill|sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which was introduced along with them, to ancient Shintoism. With the advent of the noble ghost faith, the onmyōdō became even more diverse. For example, spells such as {{ill|Katatagae|lt=changing the direction for good fortune|ja|方違え}} and {{ill|Monoimi|lt=self-consecration|ja|物忌み}}, rituals such as the Festival of the [[Dongyue Dadi|Great Emperor of the Sacred Mountain of the East]], and [[Yu the Great|Uho]] steps (hempai<ref name="kosaka">{{cite magazine |last=Kosaka |first=Shinji |date=6 October 2001 |title=陰陽師が反閇をつとめるとはどういうことか |trans-title=What does it mean for an onmyōji to do hempai? |url=https://ddnavi.com/davinci/backnumber/90/ |magazine=da Vinci |language=ja |location=[[Chiyoda, Tokyo|Chiyoda City]], Tokyo Metropolis, Japan |publisher=[[Kadokawa Future Publishing]] |volume=October, 2001}}</ref>), which were often seen in onmyōdō practices, originated from Taoism, and rice scattering and [[Norito|liturgical incantations]] {{Citation needed span|originated from ancient Shinto.|date=September 2011}} Furthermore, in the process of the [[Hokke (Fujiwara)|Hokke]] of the [[Fujiwara clan]]'s expansion and establishment of power in the Imperial court, political conflicts among nobles intensified, and there were many occasions when onmyōdō was used for slander and defamation aimed at the downfall of rival forces.
This trend became more pronounced with the rise of [[Fujiwara no Yoshifusa]] during the reigns of Emperors [[Emperor Ninmyō|Ninmyō]] and [[Emperor Montoku|Montoku]] (in the middle of the 9th century). [[Emperor Uda]] himself was well versed in the art of I Ching, and [[Fujiwara no Morosuke]] even wrote his own books "Kujō Dono Ikai" and "Kujō Nenchū Gyōji," and presented a guide that incorporated many taboos and manners based on the yin-and-yang philosophy. This environment produced charismatic onmyōji such as {{ill|Shigeoka no Kawahito|ja|滋岳川人}} and {{ill|Yuge no Koreo|ja|弓削是雄}}, and also led to the introduction of a [[regnal year]] following disasters as predicted by the [[Chinese classics]] scholar [[Miyoshi Kiyotsura]], which became a regular event after 901. As a result, onmyōdō became more and more important to the Imperial court. In addition, the fact that people outside the Bureau of Onmyō, such as Fujiwara no Morosuke and Miyoshi Kiyotsura, had mastered astrology, onmyōdō, I Ching, and calendars shows that the classified information policy under the ritsuryō system, which prohibited the leakage of onmyōdō outside the Bureau of Onmyō, had already practically failed by this time.
After the middle of the Heian period, the ritsuryō system was further loosened due to the monopolization of politics by [[Sesshō and Kampaku|Imperial Regents and Chief Imperial Advisors]], and the spread of the [[Shōen|manor system]]. As a result, informal onmyōji, who were not regular government officials and belonged to the Bureau of Onmyō, began to privately associate with the nobles, divining their good and bad fortune, and secretly performing rituals to ward off evil. In some cases, they would even undertake to kill their opponents with curses. Even among the official onmyōji who belonged to the Bureau of Onmyō, there were many who followed this trend. Their behavior was far removed from the duties of onmyōji as originally stipulated by the ritsuryō system. The onmyōji arbitrarily imposed good and bad fortune on the Emperor, Imperial family, senior government positions, and nobles in relation to the fortunate directions, and the movements of the stars, and even entered into the management of their private lives. As onmyōji began to control the spiritual world at the center of the Imperial court, they gradually went beyond their regular duties under the governmental system and began to work behind the scenes of the government. At the same time, there appeared {{ill|Kamo no Tadayuki|ja|賀茂忠行}} and his son, [[Kamo no Yasunori]], as well as their disciple, [[Abe no Seimei]], who were onmyōji well versed in all aspects of astrology, onmyōdō and calendars. They defied precedent and were promoted to even higher ranks, earning the trust of the Imperial court. Kamo no Yasunori taught calendar to his son {{ill|Kamo no Mitsuyoshi|ja|賀茂光栄}} and astrology to his disciple Abe no Seimei. They passed on these knowledge and skills only to the children of their own families and forbade teaching them to others. The astrology of the Abe family took on the nature of preaching disasters and good omens, while the calendars of the Kamo family took on a strong astrological flavor. For this reason, only the Kamo and Abe families produced onmyōji. When Abe no Seimei's grandson, Abe no Akichika, became the Head of Onmyō, he expressed his policy of always appointing people from the Kamo family as Doctor of Calendar and people from the Abe family as Doctor of Astrology. After that, the two families almost monopolized other positions in the Bureau of Onmyō that were not originally meant to be inherited by the two families. In addition, even though their actual status was that of onmyōji, they came to hold other higher official positions beyond the duties of the Bureau of Onmyō, and the bureau as a governmental system became a complete skeleton. The onmyōji became a charismatic spiritual ruler in the Imperial court with a strong tinge of religious spells and rituals, and came to wield a powerful influence. From the middle of the Heian period onward, onmyōji had a great influence on the central government of the Imperial court, from political management and personnel decisions to the abdication of the Emperor. Onmyōji were also deeply involved in the 901 incident in which [[Fujiwara no Tokihira]], the [[Minister of the Left]], relegated [[Sugawara no Michizane]] from the position of [[Minister of the Right]] to the position of deputy commissioner of [[Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]], the regional government in [[Chikuzen Province]].
It was also around this time that many onmyōji began to be seen in local areas outside of Heian-kyō, the capital at the time, although their activities as onmyōji outside of the Bureau of Onmyō were originally prohibited by the ritsuryō system. In the local areas, many private onmyōji appeared, including the {{ill|Dōma Hōshi|lt=Reverend Dōma|ja|道摩法師}} (well known as Ashiya Dōman).
Throughout the middle and latter half of the Heian period ([[11th century|11th]] to [[12th century|12th centuries]]), the Abe family produced many masters in astrology, which was the most difficult of all the duties of the Bureau of Onmyō, and the Abe family always succeeded as a Head of Onmyō, while the Kamo family succeeded as a Vice Head of Onmyō. At the time of the [[Genpei War]] at the end of the Heian period, Abe no Yasuchika, the grandson of {{ill|Abe no Yoshihira|ja|安倍吉平}}, the son of Abe no Seimei, and Abe no Suehiro, the son of {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}, held particularly high official ranks. However, due to the loss of political power that accompanied the subsequent transfer of power to the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the turmoil within the Abe family caused by the struggle for power between the [[Northern Court|Northern]] and [[Southern Court]]s at the end of the [[Kamakura period]], and the subsequent disorder during the [[Nanboku-chō period]], the power of the Abe family temporarily declined.
==In media== === Books === * ''[[Teito Monogatari]]'', written by [[Hiroshi Aramata]], published in 1985 * ''[[Onmyōji (novel series)|Onmyōji]]'', written by [[Baku Yumemakura]], published in 1988 * ''[[The Summer of the Ubume]]'', ''[[Mōryō no Hako]]'' and other Kyōgokudō series, written by [[Natsuhiko Kyogoku]], published in 1994 * ''{{ill|Onmyō no Miyako|ja|陰陽ノ京}}'', written by [[Soichiro Watase]], published in 2001 * ''[[Shōnen Onmyōji]]'', written by [[Mitsuru Yūki]], published in 2001 * ''[[Rental Magica]]'', written by {{ill|Makoto Sanda|ja|三田誠}}, published in 2004 * ''{{ill|Bakemonogatari|ja|化物語}}'', written by [[Nisio Isin]], published in 2005 * ''[[Fairy Navigator Runa]]'', written by {{ill|Miyoko Ikeda|ja|池田美代子}}, published in 2008 * ''[[Tokyo Ravens]]'', written by {{ill|Kōhei Azano|ja|あざの耕平}}, published in 2010
=== Manga and anime ===
* ''[[Tokyo Babylon]]'', written by [[Clamp (manga artists)|Clamp]], published in 1990 * ''[[X (manga)|X]]'', written by Clamp, published in 1992 * ''[[Shaman King]]'', written by [[Hiroyuki Takei]], published in 1998 * ''[[Gag Manga Biyori]]'', written by [[Kōsuke Masuda]], published in 2000 * ''[[Magical Shopping Arcade Abenobashi]]'', created by [[Gainax]], published in 2001 * ''[[Amatsuki]]'', written by {{ill|Shinobu Takayama|ja|高山しのぶ}}, published in 2002 * ''[[Gin Tama]]'', written by [[Hideaki Sorachi]], published in 2003 * ''[[xxxHolic]]'', written by Clamp, published in 2003 * ''[[Onmyō Taisenki]]'', written by Yoshihiko Tomizawa, published in 2003 * ''[[Musashi Gundoh]]'', based on an unused story by [[Monkey Punch]], published in 2006 * ''{{ill|Shibariya Komachi|ja|縛り屋小町}}'', written by [[Mick Takeuchi]], published in 2006 * ''[[Nura: Rise of the Yokai Clan]]'', written by [[Hiroshi Shiibashi]], published in 2008 * ''[[Twin Star Exorcists]]'', written by {{ill|Yoshiaki Sukeno|ja|助野嘉昭}}, published in 2013 * ''[[The Reincarnation of the Strongest Exorcist in Another World]]'', written by Kiichi Kosuzu, published in 2018 * ''[[MAO (manga)|MAO]]'', written by [[Rumiko Takahashi]], published in 2019
=== Films === *''[[Tokyo: The Last Megalopolis]]'', a 1988 Japanese fantasy film directed by [[Akio Jissōji]], based on the novel ''[[Teito Monogatari]]'' by Hiroshi Aramata *[[Onmyōji (film)|''Onmyōji'' (film)]], a 2001 Japanese film based on the [[Onmyōji (novel series)|''Onmyōji'' (novel series)]], by Baku Yumemakura and released in the U.S. in 2003 as ''Onmyōji - The Yin Yang Master'' '''''[[Onmyōji 2]]'', the 2003 sequel *''[[The Yin-Yang Master: Dream of Eternity]]'', a 2020 Chinese fantasy film based on the [[Onmyōji (novel series)|''Onmyōji'' (novel series)]], by Baku Yumemakura directed by [[Guo Jingming]], and starring [[Mark Chao]] and [[Deng Lun]] *''[[The Yinyang Master]]'', a 2021 Chinese fantasy film directed by Li Weiran, and starring [[Chen Kun]], [[Zhou Xun]] and [[William Chan]] based on the game that was based on the [[Onmyōji (novel series)|''Onmyōji'' (novel series)]], by Baku Yumemakura
=== Video games === * ''{{ill|Kuon no Kizuna|ja|久遠の絆}}'', developed by [[FOG Inc.]], released in 1998 * ''[[Tokyo Majin Gakuen]]'', produced by {{ill|Shūhō Imai|ja|今井秋芳}}, released in 1998 * ''[[Kuon]]'', developed by [[FromSoftware]], released in 2004 * ''[[11eyes: Tsumi to Batsu to Aganai no Shōjo]]'', developed by {{ill|Lass (video game developer)|lt=Lass|ja|Lass}}, released in 2008 * ''[[Naraka: Bladepoint]]'', developed by [[24 Entertainment]], released in 2021 features the character [[Kurumi Tsuchimikado]], who according to game lore is the first female Onmyōji, descended from a long line of Onmyōdō masters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.narakathegame.com/index.html|title = Naraka: Bladepoint - New Hero Out Now!}}</ref>

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[Esoteric Buddhist astrology]] to [[Sukuyōdō]]
revision.text:
{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}} {{Reading_kana|'''Shukuyōdō'''|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Lunar mansions|ja|二十八宿}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Shukuyōkyō|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Shukuyōkyō|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} * {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}} * {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり}}
== External Links == * {{kotobank|2=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|宿曜道}} * [https://yakumoin.net/宿曜占星術 宿曜占星術 八雲院] * [https://fs-astrology-members.com/宿曜占星術 宿曜占星術+news] * [https://syukuyo.com/宿曜秘宝協会 宿曜秘宝協会] * [https://kosei-do.co.jp/fortune/uranai.php 宿曜占星術 光晴堂]

* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} * {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}} * {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり}}

The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Shukuyōkyō|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} * {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}} * {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり}}

{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Lunar mansions|ja|二十八宿}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.

In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.

{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Lunar mansions|ja|二十八宿}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} * {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}} * {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり}}
== External Links == * {{kotobank|2=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|宿曜道}} * [https://yakumoin.net/宿曜占星術 宿曜占星術 八雲院] * [https://fs-astrology-members.com/宿曜占星術 宿曜占星術+news] * [https://syukuyo.com/宿曜秘宝協会 宿曜秘宝協会] * [https://kosei-do.co.jp/fortune/uranai.php 宿曜占星術 光晴堂]

The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.

== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} * {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}} * {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり}}

{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Lunar mansions|ja|二十八宿}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.

== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} * {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}} * {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり}}

{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Lunar mansions|ja|二十八宿}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.

It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
