* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.

revision.text:
{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Lunar mansions|ja|二十八宿}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} * {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}} * {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり}}
== External Links == * {{kotobank|2=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|宿曜道}} * [https://yakumoin.net/宿曜占星術 宿曜占星術 八雲院] * [https://fs-astrology-members.com/宿曜占星術 宿曜占星術+news] * [https://syukuyo.com/宿曜秘宝協会 宿曜秘宝協会] * [https://kosei-do.co.jp/fortune/uranai.php 宿曜占星術 光晴堂]

{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Lunar mansions|ja|二十八宿}}. The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.

Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.

{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Lunar mansions}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.

{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Lunar mansions}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.

The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.

* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "陰陽五行説}}" to "陰陽五行説|1=Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory}}"
revision.text:
{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説|1=Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Lunar mansions}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} * {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}} * {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり}}
== External Links == * {{kotobank|2=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|宿曜道}} * [https://yakumoin.net/宿曜占星術 宿曜占星術 八雲院] * [https://fs-astrology-members.com/宿曜占星術 宿曜占星術+news] * [https://syukuyo.com/宿曜秘宝協会 宿曜秘宝協会] * [https://kosei-do.co.jp/fortune/uranai.php 宿曜占星術 光晴堂] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Sukuyōdō-qq]] [[Category:Sukuyōdō]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Sukuyōdō-qq]] [[Category:Astrology]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Sukuyōdō-qq]] [[Category:Days of the week]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Sukuyōdō-qq]] [[Category:Esoteric Buddhism]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Sukuyōdō-qq]] [[Category:History of astronomy in Japan]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Sukuyōdō-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Sukuyōdō-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Sukuyōdō-qq]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Sukuyōdō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sukuyōdō]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Sukuyōdō]][[ja:宿曜道]]

{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説|1=Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Lunar mansions}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.

revision.text:
{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説|1=Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Lunar mansions}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} * {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}} * {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり}}
== External Links == * {{kotobank|2=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|宿曜道}} * [https://yakumoin.net/宿曜占星術 宿曜占星術 八雲院] * [https://fs-astrology-members.com/宿曜占星術 宿曜占星術+news] * [https://syukuyo.com/宿曜秘宝協会 宿曜秘宝協会] * [https://kosei-do.co.jp/fortune/uranai.php 宿曜占星術 光晴堂] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sukuyōdō]][[Category:qq]] <!--Sukuyōdō-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Sukuyōdō]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sukuyōdō]][[Category:qq]] <!--Sukuyōdō-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Astrology]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sukuyōdō]][[Category:qq]] <!--Sukuyōdō-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Days of the week]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sukuyōdō]][[Category:qq]] <!--Sukuyōdō-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Esoteric Buddhism]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sukuyōdō]][[Category:qq]] <!--Sukuyōdō-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:History of astronomy in Japan]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sukuyōdō]][[Category:qq]] <!--Sukuyōdō-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sukuyōdō]][[Category:qq]] <!--Sukuyōdō-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sukuyōdō]][[Category:qq]] <!--Sukuyōdō-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Sukuyōdō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sukuyōdō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sukuyōdō]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Sukuyōdō]][[ja:宿曜道]]

revision.text:
{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説|1=Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Lunar mansions}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} * {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}} * {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり}}

In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.

revision.text:
{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説|1=Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Lunar mansions}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} * {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}} * {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり}}

{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. {{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説|1=Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Lunar mansions}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.

{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説|1=Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Lunar mansions}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.

{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and the Five Phases|ja|陰陽五行説|1=Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Lunar mansions}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} * {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}} * {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり}}

{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 1]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|ja|陰陽五行説|1=Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Lunar mansions}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} * {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}} * {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり}}

{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|ja|陰陽五行説|1=Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Lunar mansions}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} * {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}} * {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり}}

* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.

{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|ja|陰陽五行説|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Lunar mansions}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.

{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}
{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of {{ill|astrology|ja|占星術}} introduced to {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by monks studying abroad, including {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a branch of {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of {{ill|Indian astrology|ja|インド占星術}} (a fusion of {{ill|Western astrology|ja|西洋占星術}} and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|ja|陰陽五行説|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}, {{ill|Navagraha|ja|九曜}}, {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Lunar mansions}}. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏}} by {{ill|Ichigyō|ja|一行}} emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an {{ill|Acharya|ja|阿闍梨}}'s abilities.
Monks such as {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}}, and {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}} further developed its study. In {{ill|957|ja|957}} ({{ill|Tento era|ja|天徳}} 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} from {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越}}, intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵}} in {{ill|Ōwa era|ja|応和}} 1 ({{ill|963|ja|963}}) debated {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}} over the use of auspicious dates for {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the {{ill|Genji Monogatari|ja|源氏物語}}, a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes {{ill|Hikaru Genji|ja|光源氏}}’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoritsune|ja|藤原頼経}} employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} * {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}} * {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}} * {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり}}
== External Links == * {{kotobank|2=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|宿曜道}} * [https://yakumoin.net/宿曜占星術 宿曜占星術 八雲院] * [https://fs-astrology-members.com/宿曜占星術 宿曜占星術+news] * [https://syukuyo.com/宿曜秘宝協会 宿曜秘宝協会] * [https://kosei-do.co.jp/fortune/uranai.php 宿曜占星術 光晴堂]

== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術|12=draft}} * {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|12=draft}} * {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=draft}} * {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり|12=draft}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2019-10-07}} {{読み仮名|'''宿曜道'''|すくようどう}}とは、[[平安時代]]、[[空海]]をはじめとする留学僧らにより、[[密教]]の一分野として[[日本]]へもたらされた[[占星術]]の一種。'''密教占星術'''、'''宿曜占星術'''などともいう。
== 概要 == その内容は、[[インド占星術]]（ギリシャ由来の[[西洋占星術]]とインド古来の月占星術が習合し独自に発展したもの）、[[道教]]由来の天体神信仰、[[陰陽五行説]]等が習合した雑多なものである。基本的に、[[北斗七星]]・[[九曜]]・[[十二宮]]・[[二十七宿]]または[[二十八宿]]などの天体の動きや[[七曜]]の[[曜日]]の巡りによってその[[直日]]を定め、それが凶であった場合は、その星の神々を祀る事によって運勢を好転させようとする。
所依の教典は、『[[宿曜経]]』・『[[梵天火羅九曜]]』・『[[七曜星辰別行法]]』などである。[[三九秘宿]]という独特の技法があり、これを簡略化したものが、一般に「宿曜占星術」として流布している。
[[密教]]では、造像・修法・灌頂などを行う際には吉日良辰を選ぶこととされており、[[一行]]の『[[大日経疏]]』では、吉日良辰の選定は[[阿闍梨]]の資質が問われる大切な作業とされていた。
そのために、[[空海]]・[[円仁]]・[[円珍]]らが『宿曜経』を日本に請来し、[[仁観]]が深く研究した。[[957年]]（[[天徳 (日本)|天徳]]元年）、[[日延]]が[[呉越]]より[[符天暦]]を持ち帰ったことによりその研究が盛んになり、[[法蔵 (日本)|法蔵]]が[[応和]]元年（[[963年]]）に時の[[村上天皇]]の[[御本命供]]の期日を巡って[[陰陽道]]の[[賀茂保憲]]と論争を行っており、この時期に日本の宿曜道が確立したと見られている。なお、『[[二中歴]]』では法蔵をもって日本の宿曜道の祖としている。こうした経緯から宿曜師は密教僧である例が多く、誕生月日などを元にして星占いを行ってその結果を記した「宿曜勘文」を作成したり、[[長徳]]元年（[[995年]]）には、[[興福寺]]の[[仁宗 (僧)|仁宗]]に対して[[陰陽寮]]が教える[[暦道]]と共同で暦を作成するようにという「造暦宣旨」が下されている（興福寺は[[法相宗]]であるが、この時代には[[真言宗]]との関係も強かったとされる）。しかし、この頃輸入された[[符天暦]]の計算方法を取り入れた宿曜道側が、[[長暦]]2年（[[1038年]]）に暦道を批判し、造暦から撤退してしまったが、以後も[[日食]]・[[月食]]の発生日時や[[大月]]・[[小月]]や[[閏月]]を巡って暦道と激しく争った。また、宿曜勘文などや[[星まつり|星供]]・祭供などの祈祷の奉仕を通じて権力者と結びついて、[[法隆寺]]や[[西大寺 (奈良市)|西大寺]]などの[[別当]]に任命される者も出ている。平安時代後期には[[能算]]・[[明算 (宿曜師)|明算]]父子が活躍して[[白河天皇]]や[[摂関家]]に仕え、続く平安時代末期には[[天台宗]]の流れを汲む[[珍賀]]と興福寺及び真言宗の流れを汲む[[慶算]]という2名の優れた宿曜師が出現して互いに技術を磨きながら権力者と連携して勢力を争い、一族・門人によって流派が形成される程であった。だが、[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]以後の貴族社会の衰退とともに宿曜道も没落の道を辿り、[[長寛]]3年（[[1165年]]）に珍賀が創建して宿曜道の拠点となった[[北斗降臨院]]が[[応永]]24年（[[1417年]]）に焼失すると、以後歴史から姿を消すこととなる。
== その他 == * 『[[源氏物語]]』「桐壺」にも、主人公・[[光源氏]]が誕生した際、宿曜師（「宿曜のかしこき道の人」）にその運命を占わせる場面が出てくる。 * 鎌倉期の[[藤原頼経]]は、[[珍誉]]という宿曜師を重用しており、[[御所]]地の選定も行わせている<ref>『歴史読本 5月号 特集 徹底検証!黒田官兵衛』 [[新人物往来社]] 2013年 p.274 </ref>。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * [[山下克明]]「宿曜道の形成と展開」（『平安時代の宗教文化と陰陽道』（岩田書院、1996年） ISBN 978-4-900697-65-2 所収）
== 関連項目 == * [[宿曜占星術]] * [[陰陽道]] * [[符天暦]] * [[禄命]] * [[星まつり]]
== 外部リンク == * {{kotobank|2=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|宿曜道}}
{{星座占い}} {{Normdaten}}[https://yakumoin.net/ 宿曜占星術 八雲院]
[https://fs-astrology-members.com/ 宿曜占星術+news｜空海が広めた人の統計学]
[https://syukuyo.com/ 宿曜秘宝協会]
[https://kosei-do.co.jp/fortune/uranai.php 宿曜占星術 光晴堂]
{{DEFAULTSORT:すくようとう}} [[Category:宿曜道|*]] [[Category:占星術]] [[Category:曜日]] [[Category:密教]] [[Category:日本の天文学史]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sukuyōdō]][[ja:宿曜道]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11455623]]
Wikidata name: Sukuyodo
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}

{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of [[:en:Astrology|astrology]] introduced to [[:en:Japan|Japan]] during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by monks studying abroad, including [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], as a branch of [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."

== Overview ==

The practice blends elements of [[:en:Hindu astrology|Indian astrology]] (a fusion of [[:en:Western astrology|Western astrology]] and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|ja|陰陽五行説|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 陰陽五行思想}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the [[:en:Big Dipper|Big Dipper]], [[:en:Navagraha|Navagraha]], {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zodiac|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サイン (占星術)|1=occidental astrological sign|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1795024}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakshatra|qq=|1=Nakshatra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11371366}} or [[:en:Twenty-Eight Mansions|Lunar mansions]]. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.

The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sutra of Sukuyokyo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文殊師利菩薩及諸仙所説吉凶時日善悪宿曜経|1=Sūtra on the Astrology of Auspicious and Inauspicious Days and Good and Evil Spoken by Mañjuśrī Bodhisattva and Celestial Beings|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500028}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Brahma Agni Sutra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankū Hikkyū|qq=|1=Sankū Hikkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354168}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."

In [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]], selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by [[:en:Yi Xing|Ichigyō]] emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an [[:en:Acharya|Acharya]]'s abilities.

Monks such as [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]], and [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sutra of Sukuyokyo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文殊師利菩薩及諸仙所説吉凶時日善悪宿曜経|1=Sūtra on the Astrology of Auspicious and Inauspicious Days and Good and Evil Spoken by Mañjuśrī Bodhisattva and Celestial Beings|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500028}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninkan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} further developed its study. In [[:en:957|957]] ([[:en:Tiande|Tento era]] 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichien|qq=}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}} from [[:en:Wuyue|Wuyue]], intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōzō (Japanese monk)|qq=|1=Hōzō (Japanese monk)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555339}} in [[:en:Ōwa|Ōwa era]] 1 ([[:en:963|963]]) debated [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]] over the use of auspicious dates for [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]]’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.

== Legacy ==

* In the [[:en:The Tale of Genji|Genji Monogatari]], a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes [[:en:Hikaru Genji|Hikaru Genji]]’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer.
* During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Kujō Yoritsune|Fujiwara no Yoritsune]] employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinhyo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunate|qq=|1=gosho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489051}}.

== References ==
{{reflist}}

== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Shukuyō astrology|ja|宿曜占星術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyō astrology|qq=|1=Shukuyō astrology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11455622}}
* [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]
* {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}
* {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Star Festival|ja|星まつり|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Star Festival|qq=}}

== External Links ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sukuyōdō]][[ja:宿曜道]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11455623]]
Wikidata name: Sukuyodo
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}

{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of [[:en:Astrology|astrology]] introduced to [[:en:Japan|Japan]] during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by monks studying abroad, including [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], as a branch of [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."

== Overview ==

The practice blends elements of [[:en:Hindu astrology|Indian astrology]] (a fusion of [[:en:Western astrology|Western astrology]] and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|ja|陰陽五行説|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 陰陽五行思想}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the [[:en:Big Dipper|Big Dipper]], [[:en:Navagraha|Navagraha]], {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zodiac|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サイン (占星術)|1=occidental astrological sign|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1795024}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakshatra|qq=|1=Nakshatra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11371366}} or [[:en:Twenty-Eight Mansions|Lunar mansions]]. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.

The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sutra of Sukuyokyo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文殊師利菩薩及諸仙所説吉凶時日善悪宿曜経|1=Sūtra on the Astrology of Auspicious and Inauspicious Days and Good and Evil Spoken by Mañjuśrī Bodhisattva and Celestial Beings|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500028}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Brahma Agni Sutra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankū Hikkyū|qq=|1=Sankū Hikkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354168}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."

In [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]], selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by [[:en:Yi Xing|Ichigyō]] emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an [[:en:Acharya|Acharya]]'s abilities.

Monks such as [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]], and [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sutra of Sukuyokyo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文殊師利菩薩及諸仙所説吉凶時日善悪宿曜経|1=Sūtra on the Astrology of Auspicious and Inauspicious Days and Good and Evil Spoken by Mañjuśrī Bodhisattva and Celestial Beings|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500028}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninkan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} further developed its study. In [[:en:957|957]] ([[:en:Tiande|Tento era]] 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichien|qq=}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}} from [[:en:Wuyue|Wuyue]], intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōzō (Japanese monk)|qq=|1=Hōzō (Japanese monk)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555339}} in [[:en:Ōwa|Ōwa era]] 1 ([[:en:963|963]]) debated [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]] over the use of auspicious dates for [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]]’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.

== Legacy ==

* In the [[:en:The Tale of Genji|Genji Monogatari]], a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes [[:en:Hikaru Genji|Hikaru Genji]]’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer.
* During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Kujō Yoritsune|Fujiwara no Yoritsune]] employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinhyo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunate|qq=|1=gosho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489051}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sukuyōdō]][[ja:宿曜道]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11455623]]
Wikidata name: Sukuyodo
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Cite_sources|date=2019-10-07}}

{{nihongo|Shukuyōdō|宿曜道}} is a type of [[:en:Astrology|astrology]] introduced to [[:en:Japan|Japan]] during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by monks studying abroad, including [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], as a branch of [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]. It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."

== Overview ==

The practice blends elements of [[:en:Hindu astrology|Indian astrology]] (a fusion of [[:en:Western astrology|Western astrology]] and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|ja|陰陽五行説|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 陰陽五行思想}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the [[:en:Big Dipper|Big Dipper]], [[:en:Navagraha|Navagraha]], {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zodiac|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サイン (占星術)|1=occidental astrological sign|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1795024}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakshatra|1=Nakshatra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11371366}} or [[:en:Twenty-Eight Mansions|Lunar mansions]]. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.

The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文殊師利菩薩及諸仙所説吉凶時日善悪宿曜経|1=Sūtra on the Astrology of Auspicious and Inauspicious Days and Good and Evil Spoken by Mañjuśrī Bodhisattva and Celestial Beings|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500028}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Brahma Agni Sutra|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankū Hikkyū|1=Sankū Hikkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354168}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."

In [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]], selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by [[:en:Yi Xing|Ichigyō]] emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an [[:en:Acharya|Acharya]]'s abilities.

Monks such as [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]], and [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文殊師利菩薩及諸仙所説吉凶時日善悪宿曜経|1=Sūtra on the Astrology of Auspicious and Inauspicious Days and Good and Evil Spoken by Mañjuśrī Bodhisattva and Celestial Beings|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500028}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} further developed its study. In [[:en:957|957]] ([[:en:Tiande|Tento era]] 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichien|qq=}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}} from [[:en:Wuyue|Wuyue]], intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōzō (Japanese monk)|1=Hōzō (Japanese monk)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555339}} in [[:en:Ōwa|Ōwa era]] 1 ([[:en:963|963]]) debated [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]] over the use of auspicious dates for [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]]’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.

== Legacy ==

* In the [[:en:The Tale of Genji|Genji Monogatari]], a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes [[:en:Hikaru Genji|Hikaru Genji]]’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer.
* During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Kujō Yoritsune|Fujiwara no Yoritsune]] employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinhyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunate|1=gosho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489051}}.

It is also known as "Esoteric astrology" or "Shukuyō astrology."
== Overview ==
The practice blends elements of [[:en:Hindu astrology|Indian astrology]] (a fusion of [[:en:Western astrology|Western astrology]] and ancient Indian lunar astrology), Taoist celestial deity worship, and {{ill|Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|ja|陰陽五行説|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 陰陽五行思想}}. It determines auspicious and inauspicious days based on the movements of celestial bodies such as the [[:en:Big Dipper|Big Dipper]], [[:en:Navagraha|Navagraha]], {{ill|Zodiac|ja|十二宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zodiac|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サイン (占星術)|1=occidental astrological sign|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1795024}}, and lunar mansions like the {{ill|Nakshatra|ja|二十七宿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakshatra|1=Nakshatra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11371366}} or [[:en:Twenty-Eight Mansions|Lunar mansions]]. When an inauspicious day is identified, practitioners would perform rituals to honor the deities associated with those stars to improve fortune.
The foundational scriptures include the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文殊師利菩薩及諸仙所説吉凶時日善悪宿曜経|1=Sūtra on the Astrology of Auspicious and Inauspicious Days and Good and Evil Spoken by Mañjuśrī Bodhisattva and Celestial Beings|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500028}}, {{ill|Brahma Agni Sutra|ja|梵天火羅九曜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Brahma Agni Sutra|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja|七曜星辰別行法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shichi Yō Seishin Betsu Gōhō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. One unique technique is {{ill|Sankū Hikkyū|ja|三九秘宿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankū Hikkyū|1=Sankū Hikkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354168}}, which has a simplified version popularized as "Shukuyō astrology."
In [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]], selecting auspicious dates for rituals such as statue construction, ceremonies, or consecration was a crucial task. The {{ill|Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja|大日経疏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahāvairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by [[:en:Yi Xing|Ichigyō]] emphasized the importance of this process as a test of an [[:en:Acharya|Acharya]]'s abilities.
Monks such as [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]], and [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] brought the {{ill|Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja|宿曜経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sutra of Sukuyokyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文殊師利菩薩及諸仙所説吉凶時日善悪宿曜経|1=Sūtra on the Astrology of Auspicious and Inauspicious Days and Good and Evil Spoken by Mañjuśrī Bodhisattva and Celestial Beings|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500028}} to Japan, and {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} further developed its study. In [[:en:957|957]] ([[:en:Tiande|Tento era]] 1), {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichien|qq=}} brought the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}} from [[:en:Wuyue|Wuyue]], intensifying research. {{ill|Hōzō (Japanese monk)|ja|法蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōzō (Japanese monk)|1=Hōzō (Japanese monk)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555339}} in [[:en:Ōwa|Ōwa era]] 1 ([[:en:963|963]]) debated [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]] over the use of auspicious dates for [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]]’s offerings. This marked the formal establishment of Shukuyōdō in Japan.
== Legacy ==
* In the [[:en:The Tale of Genji|Genji Monogatari]], a scene in the "Kiritsubo" chapter describes [[:en:Hikaru Genji|Hikaru Genji]]’s fate being predicted by a Shukuyō astrologer. * During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Kujō Yoritsune|Fujiwara no Yoritsune]] employed a Shukuyō practitioner, {{ill|Chinhyo|ja|珍誉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinhyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, to select auspicious locations for the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|御所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunate|1=gosho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489051}}.
